source: Constitution of India, Article 21 — Right to Shelter
title: Right to life includes right to shelter — eviction and housing rights
text: The Supreme Court of India has interpreted Article 21 of the Constitution — the right to life and personal liberty — to include the right to shelter. In Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985), the Supreme Court held that the right to livelihood is included in the right to life under Article 21, and that eviction from a dwelling without following due process of law violates Article 21. A person cannot be evicted from their home without being given an opportunity to be heard. The procedure for eviction must be fair, just, and reasonable. Forced eviction by a landlord without a court order violates the tenant's right to life under Article 21. Cutting off essential utilities like water and electricity to force someone to vacate their home is a violation of the right to live with dignity under Article 21. The right to shelter does not mean that a person can never be evicted, but it means that eviction must follow due process — proper notice, an opportunity to contest the eviction in court, and a valid court order. These constitutional protections apply to all tenants regardless of whether they have a written lease, regardless of their economic status, and regardless of whether they are in arrears of rent. Any eviction that bypasses judicial process can be challenged in the High Court through a writ petition under Article 226.
